answer:
First, we'll simplify the expression inside the parentheses using the difference of two squares identity:

$$
\sqrt{a^{2}-b^{2}}=\left|a-b\right|
$$

Applying this identity to our expression:

$$
\sqrt{n^{3}-3}-\sqrt{n^{3}-2} = \left|n^{3}-3-\left(n^{3}-2\right)\right|
$$

$$
= \left|n^{3}-3-n^{3}+2\right|
$$

$$
= \left|n^{3}-n^{3}+1-3\right|
$$

$$
= \left|-2\right|
$$

$$
= \boxed{2}
$$

Now, we'll substitute this value back into the original expression:

$$
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sqrt{n(n+1)(n+2)}\left(\sqrt{n^{3}-3}-\sqrt{n^{3}-2}\right)
$$

$$
= \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sqrt{n(n+1)(n+2)} \cdot 2
$$

$$
= \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} 2\sqrt{n(n+1)(n+2)}
$$

To find the limit, we can factor out $2n$:

$$
= \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} 2n\sqrt{\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{n}}
$$

$$
= 2\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} n\sqrt{\frac{(n+1)(n+2)}{n}}
$$

$$
= 2\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} n\sqrt{\frac{n+1}{n} + \frac{n+2}{n}}
$$

$$
= 2\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} n\left(\sqrt{1+\frac{1}{n}} + \sqrt{1+\frac{2}{n}}\right)
$$

$$
= 2\left(\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sqrt{1+\frac{1}{n}} + \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sqrt{1+\frac{2}{n}}\right)
$$

Now, we'll use the limit of the sum of two sequences:

$$
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \left(a_{n}+b_{n}\right) = \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} a_{n} + \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} b_{n}
$$

$$
= 2\left(\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sqrt{1+\frac{1}{n}} + \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sqrt{1+\frac{2}{n}}\right)
$$

$$
= 2\left(\sqrt{\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} 1+\frac{1}{n}} + \sqrt{\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} 1+\frac{2}{n}}\right)
$$

$$
= 2\left(\sqrt{1+\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n}} + \sqrt{1+\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{2}{n}}\right)
$$

$$
= 2\left(\sqrt{1+0} + \sqrt{1+0}\right)
$$

$$
= 2\left(1+1\right)
$$

$$
= \boxed{4}
$$
